title: Code for Virally Encoded CRISPR Screen - a tool for dissecting Virus Host interactions
author: Finkel, Yaara
license_note: Zenodo record metadata license field: cc-by-4.0
description: CRISPR-Cas9 technology has transformed the study of gene function, enabling systematic interrogation of host-virus interactions. However, most CRISPR-based screens in the context of viral infections rely on cell survival as a readout, limiting their sensitivity and biasing results toward early infection stages. To address these challenges, we developed VECOS (Virus Encoded CRISPR-based direct readOut System), a virus-centric approach in which human cytomegalovirus (HCMV) is engineered to express single-guide RNA (sgRNA) libraries directly from its genome. This system allows sgRNA abundance, embedded in the viral genome, to serve as a direct and quantitative readout of gene perturbation effects on viral propagation. By tracking sgRNA levels at distinct stages of the viral infection cycle, VECOS enables a detailed, multidimensional analysis of host-virus interactions. Here, we present a modular  detailed protocol for: 1. Constructing and reconstituting complex sgRNA libraries in double-stranded DNA (dsDNA) viruses using bacterial artificial chromosomes (BACs). 2. Performing multi-passage screens to investigate perturbation effects on various stages of viral infection. And 3. Analyzing the multi-passage and multi-stages sgRNA abundance measurements utilizing a comprehensive framework for data analysis. Successful implementation of this full protocol takes 14-22 weeks and requires proficiency in molecular biology, as well as basic familiarity with Unix-based computing and programming in R for data processing . This method offers researchers a robust tool to uncover the molecular mechanisms driving viral propagation and host-virus interactions.  The code provided here enables performing QC and statistical analysis of longitudinal screen data with multiple selection stages.
steps_raw: CRISPR-Cas9 technology has transformed the study of gene function, enabling systematic interrogation of host-virus interactions. However, most CRISPR-based screens in the context of viral infections rely on cell survival as a readout, limiting their sensitivity and biasing results toward early infection stages. To address these challenges, we developed VECOS (Virus Encoded CRISPR-based direct readOut System), a virus-centric approach in which human cytomegalovirus (HCMV) is engineered to express single-guide RNA (sgRNA) libraries directly from its genome. This system allows sgRNA abundance, embedded in the viral genome, to serve as a direct and quantitative readout of gene perturbation effects on viral propagation. By tracking sgRNA levels at distinct stages of the viral infection cycle, VECOS enables a detailed, multidimensional analysis of host-virus interactions. Here, we present a modular  detailed protocol for: 1. Constructing and reconstituting complex sgRNA libraries in double-stranded DNA (dsDNA) viruses using bacterial artificial chromosomes (BACs). 2. Performing multi-passage screens to investigate perturbation effects on various stages of viral infection. And 3. Analyzing the multi-passage and multi-stages sgRNA abundance measurements utilizing a comprehensive framework for data analysis. Successful implementation of this full protocol takes 14-22 weeks and requires proficiency in molecular biology, as well as basic familiarity with Unix-based computing and programming in R for data processing . This method offers researchers a robust tool to uncover the molecular mechanisms driving viral propagation and host-virus interactions.  The code provided here enables performing QC and statistical analysis of longitudinal screen data with multiple selection stages.
